claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you enquiring about, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in finding out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. 

I have one child - Alex, who's 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information!

To continue assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I have a few more questions.

**Are you responsible for Alex?** This typically means Alex lives with you, or you contribute at least the same amount towards their upkeep as the Child Benefit you'd receive (currently £25.60 a week for the eldest or only child).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex doesn't actually live with me at the moment, but I do contribute towards their upkeep regularly - I help out with things like clothes, school supplies, and other bits and pieces they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining that. Since Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand the situation a bit more.

**Does Alex live with someone else, such as another family member or your ex-partner?** And if so, is that person already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with their other parent - my ex-partner. But as far as I know, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and not with you, I need to ask a couple more questions to determine eligibility.

**How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep each week?** The Child Benefit rate for an only or eldest child is currently £25.60 per week, so I need to understand if your contribution is at least this amount.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute about £26 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detail. That's helpful to know.

I have one more important question: **Does your ex-partner (the parent Alex lives with) contribute towards Alex's upkeep as well?** 

This is important because when a child lives with one person but another person contributes to their upkeep, the person the child lives with generally has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner does contribute - Alex lives with them after all, so they're providing for Alex day-to-day.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me check the specific rules about Child Benefit when a child lives with someone else to make sure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience while I checked the rules.

Based on the information you've provided, I now have a clear picture of your situation. Let me explain what the rules say:

**Key points:**
- Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child
- When a child lives with someone else, the person the child **lives with** generally has priority for claiming Child Benefit
- While contributing to a child's upkeep can allow someone to continue receiving Child Benefit (if they were already getting it before the child moved), this is different from making a new claim

Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and your ex-partner is also contributing to Alex's day-to-day care, your ex-partner would have priority for claiming Child Benefit. The rules state that you're "usually responsible for a child if you live with them" - this gives the resident parent priority.

Before I give my final assessment, I have one more question: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Based on all the information you've provided, I now have enough to make an assessment of your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you would not be eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

- **Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child**, and when two people could be considered responsible for the same child, the rules give priority to the person the child lives with.
- Since Alex lives with your ex-partner and your ex-partner is also contributing to Alex's day-to-day care, your ex-partner has priority to claim Child Benefit.
- While your contribution of £26 per week is commendable and meets the financial threshold, it doesn't override the priority given to the resident parent.

**What this means:**
- Your ex-partner would be the person eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
- If your ex-partner is not currently claiming, they may wish to consider doing so, as they would qualify.

If you believe there are any special circumstances I haven't considered, or if your situation changes in the future (for example, if Alex comes to live with you), please do revisit your eligibility. You can also contact the Child Benefit Office directly if you'd like to discuss your specific situation further.

Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives with their other parent (the claimant's ex-partner) rather than with the claimant. According to the eligibility rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child, and you are 'usually responsible for a child if you live with them'. When two people are responsible for the same child, priority is given to the person the child lives with. Although the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which meets the threshold of at least £25.60), the ex-partner with whom Alex lives is also contributing to Alex's care on a day-to-day basis. Since Alex lives with the ex-partner and the ex-partner is also contributing to Alex's upkeep, the ex-partner has priority to claim Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex in these circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and contributes £26 per week towards their child Alex's upkeep. However, Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, who also contributes to Alex's day-to-day care. Under Child Benefit rules, when a child lives with one person while another contributes to their upkeep, priority is given to the person the child lives with. Therefore, the ex-partner would have priority to claim Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant would not be eligible.

When two people are responsible for the same child, priority is given to the person the child lives with. Although the claimant contributes approximately £26 per week towards Alex's upkeep (which meets the threshold of at least £25.60), the ex-partner with whom Alex lives is also contributing to Alex's care on a day-to-day basis. Since Alex lives with the ex-partner and the ex-partner is also contributing to Alex's upkeep, the ex-partner has priority to claim Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex in these circumstances.

However, Alex lives with the claimant's ex-partner, who also contributes to Alex's day-to-day care. Under Child Benefit rules, when a child lives with one person while another contributes to their upkeep, priority is given to the person the child lives with. Therefore, the ex-partner would have priority to claim Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant would not be eligible.
